title: 2001 Suhrawardi Al Maqtul The Martyr Of
author: Roxanne D. Marcotte

MARCOTTE
Institut Français d'Études Arabes, Damasco On Friday, the 29th of dhü al-hijjah, 587 [January 17, 1192] after the hour of
prayer, the corpse of Shihàb al-Dïn al-Suhrawardï was carried out of the prison of
Aleppo, and all the partisans of that man dispersed and left him. ^
This is an excerpt of how, more than acentury after the tragic event, Ibn
Khallikan's Wafayat al-A'yan wa Abnâ' aUZamân registers the
day of Shihàb al-Dîn al-Suhrawardï'sdeath - the shaykh al-maqtûl.

Biographers
are, however, at odds regarding the exact course of events that led to
al-Suhrawardfs tragic death.

XXn, 2001 with the various accounts provided by biographers? ^ More importantly,
however, is the significance —both religious and sociopolitical— of the events
that led to the trial and execution of al-Suhrawardï.
Continued from the source text...

Others have suffered similar fate, the most famous being al-Hallàj who was
executed for heresy in 310/922 against abackground of religious and political
intrigues. ^ In 7th/13th century Syria, politically and religiously motivated trials
occurred periodically: Sayf al-Dîn Abu al-Hasan 'All al-Àmidï was dismissed from the chair he held at the madrasa al-'Aziziyya by al-Malik
al-Ashraf, the Ayyùbid ruler of Damascus, for having taught philosophy
and theology (kalâm), having been equally suspected of political treason; ^ and
during the reign of al-Sàlih Ismà'îl, ruler of Damascus, the Shâfi'î Grand Qàdî,
Rafí' al-Dïn al-Jïlï was accused of professing suspicious doctrines in the midst
of afinancial scandal ^ and suffered an end no less tragic than that of al-Hallaj,
' Ayn al-Qudat al-Hamadhànï, ^ or al-Suhrawardï.

These trials illustrate, albeit in
their extreme tragic outcome, the ties that often linked the religious milieus to the
sultan, the ultimate power of the sultan to decide of the course of events and,
finally, the sultan'sinterference in religious matters —such as in these religious
trials— to maintain order and stability. ^ which she then uses to corroborate her argument about the precarious political situation of Salih
al-Dïn in the region (242), cf.

X X N, 2 0 0 1 SUHRAWARDI AL-MAQTÜL, THE MARTYR OF ALEPPO 397 FORMATIVE YEARS Yahyà Ibn Habash Ibn Amirak Abu al-Futüh Shihàb al-Dïn al-Suhrawardï
was bom in 549/1154, in the village of Suhraward near the modem city of
Zanjan, in what is now northwestern Iran.

His early studies in philosophy and
jurispmdence —^he was said to have followed the Shâfi'ï madhhab ^— were
undertaken in Marâgha with shaykh Majd al-Dm al-Jîlî, ateacher of Fakhr al-Dïn
al-RàzL ^^ Marâgha was, at that time, acenter of leaming which was to become
famous after Hulagu, the Mongol conqueror, had an observatory built near it and
assembled the greatest astronomers of the day under the direction of Khwàja
Nasïr al-Dïn al-Tûsï.
Continued from the source text...

After some time spent in Marâgha, he is reported to have left in search of
isolation.

There he continued and completed his formal training with
Zahîr al-Fârisï, ^^ alogician with whom he read the Kitâb aUBasà 'ir al-Nâsiriyya
jï al-Mantiq ^•
^ of the logician Zayn al-Dïn 'Umar Ibn Sahlân al-Sàwî or al-Sâvajï, ^^ awork which, according to al-Suhrawardï'sown account,
^ Yâqût (Ibn 'Abd Allah al-Hamawî), Mu 'jam al-Udabà ' li-l-YâqUt, 20 vols., éd.
Continued from the source text...

Ibn Khallikàn, Wafayàt, 272 (Ibn Khallikan's, 4, 156).
ïo Yâqût, Mu'jam, 314-5; cf.

Khallikan's Wafayàt, cf.

There are discrepancies between the Arabie and the Persian translations, e.g., the Arabic text
reports that the death of al-Suhrawardî occurred at the age of 36, while the Persian translation
mentions that there are reports that he was 88, or 50.

Moreover, the Persian text contains the most
extensive bibliography of al-Suhrawardî'sworks, cf. al-Shahrazüñ, Nuzhat al-Arwàh wa Rawdat
al-Afràh «Tàrïkh al-Hukamà'», Persian trans.

travel in pursuit of knowledge.

The next period of his Ufe was marked by his acquaintance with anumber of
Sufi mastersft"omwhom he sought to acquire new knowledge.
Continued from the source text...

Al-Shahrazüñ, the
disciple, mentions how al-Suhrawardî would practice spiritual exercises (riyâdât),
harsh (even if they were brief) retreats (khalwa rashîqa), and meditation.

He set out
to live the life of atrue ascetic, as one biographer notes, «emaciated as Christ, as
an itinerant mystic» (masîh al-shakl qalandar al-sifa), engaging in severe and
extreme ascetic exercises, at times fasting up to aweek. ^^ After having traveled all
over Iran (from Marâgha to Isfahan), he then set out for AnatoHa - the then Bilad
al-Rüm and Diyâr Bakr (north of Mâridïn and present day Turko-Syrian borders).
Continued from the source text...

He then went to Syria where anumber of religious scholars like him of Iranian
origin immigrated, from the end of the 5th/llth century till the 9th/13th century. ^^
Al-Suhrawardï ended his travels in Aleppo. ^^ He mentions that this life of
wandering in search of knowledge had led him, when he was about 30 (lunar) years
old, to find no one to teach him something new. ^^ It had, on the one hand, prepared
the young al-Suhrawardi to be an independent thinker; on the other hand, it gave
him the self-assurance that was to blind him to the true consequence of the events
that were to lead to his death.
Avicenna'sal-Najàt, responding to al-Shahrastanfs criticisms (especially of logic).

XXn, 2001 SUHRAWARDÏ AL-MAQTÜL, THE MARTYR OF ALEPPO 399 It was most probably during this period of wandering that he met many Sufi
masters such as shaykh Fakhr al-Dïn al-Mâridînï in the city of the
same name ^^ and who seems to have had great respect for the lad whom he
befriended. -^^ The Nuzhat aUArwàh wa Rawdat al-Afrah of al-Shahrazùn, alater
source, reports many anecdotes about al-Suhrawardï'stotal detachment of
worldly affairs. ^^ It is also during this period —^before reaching Aleppo— that alSuhrawardï would have met such individuals as Imad al-Dïn Abu Bakr, the son
of (Fakhr al-Dïn) Qarà Arslân (adescendant of Artuq Ibn
Ekseb) and prince {amïr) of Khartpüt (Khartpert) after the death of his father, ^^
and for whom he appears to have written his Arabic al-Alwàh al- 'Imâdiyya. ^^
Shaykh Diyá' al-Dîn Ibn Saqar is said to have reported that when alSuhrawardï returned from Anatolia in 579/1183, he went to Aleppo '^^ at the time
when al-Záhir (Ghiyàth al-Dïn) al-Ghâzï Ibn Ayyûb —the son of
Salâh al-Dïn al-Ayyûbï (Saladin) who ruled over Egypt, Yemen
and Sham (roughly present day Lebanon, Syria and the whole of Palestine) - had
just been assigned the governorship of Aleppo. ^^
Upon his arrival, al-Suhrawardî went to the religious school, al-Madrasa alHaliwiyya ^^ where he attended the lessons of Iftikhar al-Dïn (al-Hàshimï), the head of the Hanafiyya school. -^^ Although belonging to the

MARCOTTE AQ, XXn, 2001 Shàfi'îte rite, al-Suhrawardî opted for al-Madrasa al-Halàwiyya where the
teaching staff included many Iranians since its foundation by Nùr al-Dïn in
544/1149. ^^ At al-Madrasa al-Halàwiyya, he debated with the jurists (juqahà')
and their students and was able to gain respect from Iftikhar al-Dîn.
Continued from the source text...

It is reported
that al-Suhrawardi:
Attended the lesson of its shaykh, the distinguished Iftikhar al-Dïn
[Halàwiyya school].

He discussed with the jurists amongst his students and
others, and debated with them on anumber of questions.
Continued from the source text...

None of them were able
to keep up [intellectually] with him; he thus gained the upper hand over them.

He became part
of his gathering, grew close [to him] and came to find his place amongst the
people [of these gatherings]. ^^
It is most probably in Aleppo that al-Suhrawardï wrote his Hikmat al-Ishràq
which he completed in 582/1186, ^^ ayear before Salah al-Dïn liberated
Jerusalem from the Franks and which, according to al-ShahrazM, corresponded
to the time when he would also have finished writing his al-MasharV wa alMutûrahàt, at the age of nearly thirty years old. ^^
Al-Suhrawardi'sstay in Aleppo —and his association with its local
indigenous religious class— would prove to be fatal.
Continued from the source text...

The growing discontent of
the local indigenous religious class towards this young and intelligent
«Easterner» was, most probably, fuelled by al-Suhrawardî'sown self-assurance.

Indication of this is found in the notices on al-Suhrawardî where biographers
have recorded that he was aman who would always come out victorious from a
debate, on account of his ability at expressing his thought in the most eloquent
fashion.
Continued from the source text...

Unfortunately, he also seems to have behaved in such amanner as to
display acondescending arrogance for the scholars of Aleppo. ^^ Could it be that 587/1191, aposition he occupied till his death, cf.

Ibn Shaddàd, Al-A'làq al-Khaûra fi Dhikr
Umarâ' al-Shàm wa al-Jazîra, 1st section, 1st part, éd.

X X I I, 2 0 0 1 SUHRAWARDÎ AL-MAQTÜL, THE MARTYR OF ALEPPO 401 al-Suhrawardfs own behavior exasperated the rehgious leaders of Aleppo who,
in retaliation, sought to have him accused and then condemned?
Continued from the source text...

Ibn AM
Usaybi'anotes, reporting from shaykh Sadid al-Dm Ibn 'Umar,
that afriendship had developed between al-Suhrawardi and the shaykh Fakhr alDîn al-Màridîm whom he would often visit.

The shaykh Fakhr al-Dîn was in a
habit of teUing his disciples that there was no one more intelligent (adhka) than
this youth, or more eloquent (afsah) and unequalled.
Continued from the source text...

Shaykh Fakhr al-Dîn,
however, exhibited real concern for the lad, whose carelessness, rashness and
unrestricted recklessness would —one day— be the cause of his downfall. ^^ This
latter aspect of al-Suhrawardî'spersonality found its way into biographical
works that note both aspects of al-Suhrawardî'scharacter.

Anecdotes in which alSuhrawardî is described as often going about dressed as apoor man ^"^ and
without any pretension are noted, together with reports about his heedlessness,
which could easily account for the fact that, with the passing of time, the anger
of his adversaries mounted to the point of causing his downfall. ^^ SOCIOPOLITICAL AND RELIGIOUS CONTEXTS Aconjunction of religious and political factors led to al-Suhrawardî's
execution that can only be understood with regards to the sociopoHtical and
rehgious context of the time.
Continued from the source text...

Aleppo was conquered by Salah al-Dîn in 579/1183,
the same year al-Suhrawardî would have reached the city.

According to
Humphreys, «Aleppo had always resisted Saladin more strongly than any other
place.
Continued from the source text...

It was far more closely tied to the Zangid dynasty than the other Syrian
towns, and both its amïrs and religious notables had aclear sense of cohesion and
local identity.

In addition its urban mihtia was still a Hvely organization...». ^^ It is,
therefore, not surprising that once the city was conquered, Salah al-Dîn tried to win
the notables and rehgious people of Aleppo by extending favors to this group. ^^
Conquered, Aleppo was given in the form of aland grant (iqtâ ') to Salah alDîn'sfourth son al-Zahir who was made sultan, although, at that time, he was ^^ Ibn Abî Usaybi'a, 'Uyün, 642.
^"^ Iftikhar al-Dïn al-Hàshimî tried in vain to offer to al-Suhrawardî beautiful clothes to replace
his rags; while in the company of al-Suhrawardï, Ibn Raqïqa, the physician, was asked what he was
doing with acaretaker of mules, cf.
Continued from the source text...

Ibn Abî Usaybi'a, 'Uyün, 644.
^^ Ibn Abî Usaybi'a, 'Uyün, 644.
^^ Humphreys, From Saladin to the Mongols, 55.
^^ cf.

XXII, 2001 only eleven years old. ^^ In the same year, but six month later, the land grant was
transferred to one of his brothers, al-'Àdil.

By 581/1185, Salih alDm was still trying to curtail the power of the Zangid and Artuqid princes and
attempting to reduce them to vassaldom. ^^ Ayear later, al-'Àdil forfited his
possession and control of Aleppo and, in what appears to have been apolitically
motivated move, exchanged it for territories in Egypt.
Continued from the source text...

In 582/1186, al-Záhir, now
fourteen years of age, was sent back to Aleppo as al-Malik al-Zahir, the ruler of
Aleppo accompanied by two experienced conamanders (amir) who were to be in
charge of administrative and military matters. ^ Meanwhile, Saláh al-Dîn was
still trying to consolidate his power over the region.

It has been suggested that al-Suhrawardi might have unwillingly exacerbated
the animosity against him by writing awork dedicated to 'Imad al-Dîn Artuqid,
ruler in Kharpüt (Khartpert) who estabUshed in 581/1185 acollateral branch of
the Artuqids.
Continued from the source text...

"^^ The Artuqids, a Turkish dynasty, had
settled in Diyâr Bakr, North of Mâridîn and the Syro-Turkish border, around
Hisn Kayfi and Àmid; acollateral branch had settled around Mâridîn and
Mayàfâriqîn.

They ruled over some territories, often as vassals, for almost three
centuries until the Ayyùbid conquest in 630/1232.
Continued from the source text...

The rise of the Zangids halted the expansion of the Artuqids who became
the vassals of Nûr al-Dîn Mahmüd b.

Zangî. ^'^ Humphreys mentions that the
Artuqids «represented more the Turkmen than the Great Saljuqid tradition and
hence were even more clearly oriented toward the political value of the
steppe...»; hence, Saláh al-Dîn had to reconcile the guiding principles of
«shared authority and local autonomy» with these local rulers in order «to
balance the ultimately irreconcilable claims of the absolutist sultanate
and collective sovereignty».
Continued from the source text...

XXII, 2001 SUHRAWARDÏ AL-MAQTÜL, THE MARTYR OF ALEPPO 419 ABSTRACT Shihàb al-Dîn al-Suhrawardfs life is obscure.

Some clues regarding his studies,
travels and encounters are found in short biographical notices of the 12th and 13th
centuries.
Continued from the source text...

These notices can provide the means to sketch al-Suhrawardfs biography: his
early life, his coming to Aleppo, the mounting opposition of the ulemas of Aleppo, and
the final moments that led to his tragic death.

The sociopolitical context of Aleppo
provides agood framework for the interpretation of the data provided by the
biographers.
Continued from the source text...

Datos aislados referentes asus
estudios, viajes ycontactos se encuentran en breves noticias de los diccionarios biográficos de los siglos XII yxin.

ulemas de Alepo ysu trágica muerte, interpretada en el marco yel contexto de la ciudad.
